name: Ganesha
shortDescription: Ganesha is the Hindu god of beginnings, wisdom, and remover of obstacles. He is widely revered as the son of Shiva and Parvati.
description: Ganesha, also known as Ganapati and Vinayaka, is one of the most beloved and widely worshipped deities in the Hindu pantheon. He is the son of Shiva and Parvati. His elephant head, large ears, and potbelly make him easily recognizable. Ganesha is revered as the god of beginnings, remover of obstacles, patron of arts and sciences, and deva of intellect and wisdom. He is invoked at the beginning of rituals and ceremonies as his presence is believed to ensure success and ward off evil. Stories surrounding his birth vary across different Puranas, but all emphasize his divine origins and unique form. One popular narrative describes how Parvati created Ganesha from the turmeric paste she used for bathing, instructing him to guard the entrance to her chambers. When Shiva returned, Ganesha, not recognizing him, refused him entry, leading to a confrontation in which Shiva severed Ganesha's head. Parvati was distraught, and Shiva, filled with remorse, promised to restore Ganesha to life. He instructed his attendants to bring the head of the first living being they encountered facing north, which happened to be an elephant. Shiva then attached the elephant head to Ganesha's body, bringing him back to life. Another version suggests that the gods and goddesses were invited to celebrate the birth of Ganesha, and Shani, the god of Saturn, refused to look at the child, warning that his gaze would harm the infant. Parvati insisted, and as soon as Shani looked at Ganesha, the infant's head was burned to ashes. Vishnu then flew to the banks of the Pushpa-Bhadra river and brought back the head of a powerful elephant. Ganesha's mount is the mouse (Mushika), symbolizing his ability to overcome any obstacle, no matter how small. He is often depicted holding various objects, each representing different aspects of his power and attributes, such as an axe (to cut away attachments), a noose (to capture difficulties), a sweet (modak) (representing reward for spiritual practice), and a lotus (symbolizing enlightenment). He is particularly associated with the festival of Ganesh Chaturthi, a ten-day celebration marking his birth.
